provisions.title: Interpretive Note to Recommendation 1 (Risk Assessment)
provisions.text: Countries should have mechanisms to provide appropriate information on the results of the risk assessment to all relevant competent authorities, self-regulatory bodies (SRBs), financial institutions and DNFBPs. Risk assessments should cover: types of ML/TF threats; types of ML/TF vulnerabilities of the financial and DNFBP sectors; the adequacy of the existing AML/CFT legal and institutional framework; and the effectiveness of AML/CFT measures. Risk assessment should be reviewed periodically and kept up to date. Supranational risk assessments (e.g. EU SNRA) should be considered as complementary inputs.

provisions.title: Interpretive Note to Recommendation 10 (CDD)
provisions.text: The CDD measures to be taken are: identifying the customer and verifying that customer's identity using reliable, independent source documents, data or information; identifying the beneficial owner and taking reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner, such that the financial institution is satisfied that it knows who the beneficial owner is; understanding and, as appropriate, obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship; and conducting ongoing due diligence on the business relationship and scrutiny of transactions to ensure consistency with the institution's knowledge of the customer. For legal persons and arrangements, the financial institution should identify and verify the identity of the customer, determine the nature of the customer's business, and identify the beneficial ownership and control structure.

provisions.title: Interpretive Note to Recommendation 15 (Virtual Assets)
provisions.text: Countries should apply the FATF standards to virtual assets (VA) and virtual asset service providers (VASPs). VASPs should be required to: be licensed or registered; comply with CDD, record-keeping, and STR requirements; implement the travel rule for VA transfers; and be subject to risk-based supervision. Countries should identify and assess ML/TF risks arising from VA activities. VASPs should be required to implement effective targeted financial sanctions measures. The FATF defines VA as a digital representation of value that can be digitally traded, or transferred, and can be used for payment or investment purposes.

provisions.title: Interpretive Note to Recommendation 24 (Beneficial Ownership)
provisions.text: Countries should establish a multi-pronged approach to beneficial ownership transparency of legal persons. This should include: a beneficial ownership register (central register or one maintained by a public authority); supplemented by information held by the company itself and by financial institutions and DNFBPs applying CDD measures. Beneficial ownership information should be adequate (sufficient to identify the natural persons who are beneficial owners), accurate (verified against reliable sources), and current (updated in a timely manner, at least annually). Competent authorities should have timely access to adequate, accurate and current beneficial ownership information. The definition of beneficial owner should cover natural persons who ultimately own or control 25% or more of the shares or voting rights, or who otherwise exercise control.

provisions.title: Travel Rule Implementation Detail
provisions.text: For wire transfers, the ordering financial institution must include: originator name, originator account number, originator address (or national identity number, customer identification number, or date and place of birth); beneficiary name and beneficiary account number. For cross-border wire transfers of USD/EUR 1,000 or more, full originator information must be transmitted. For domestic wire transfers, reduced information may be acceptable if full information can be made available to the beneficiary institution on request. The travel rule also applies to VA transfers: originator VASPs must obtain and transmit required originator and beneficiary information, and beneficiary VASPs must obtain required beneficiary information.

provisions.title: Syria - High-Risk Jurisdiction
provisions.text: Syria is identified by the FATF as a jurisdiction with strategic AML/CFT deficiencies. The FATF calls on its members and urges all jurisdictions to apply enhanced due diligence measures and counter-measures proportionate to the risks arising from the jurisdiction.

provisions.title: Philippines - Increased Monitoring
provisions.text: The Philippines is working with the FATF to address strategic deficiencies in its AML/CFT regime. Areas of focus include: casino sector supervision, beneficial ownership transparency, risk-based supervision of DNFBPs, and international cooperation.

provisions.title: Vietnam - Increased Monitoring
provisions.text: Vietnam committed to address strategic deficiencies in its AML/CFT framework. Key areas include: national risk assessment, risk-based supervision, beneficial ownership transparency, and effectiveness of law enforcement and prosecution of ML offences.

provisions.title: Croatia - Increased Monitoring
provisions.text: Croatia is working with the FATF to address identified strategic deficiencies. Priority areas include: strengthening the supervisory framework for AML/CFT compliance, improving the use of financial intelligence, and enhancing international cooperation.

provisions.title: Cameroon - Increased Monitoring
provisions.text: Cameroon is working with the FATF to address strategic deficiencies. Key focus areas include: strengthening the national AML/CFT legal framework, improving risk-based supervision, and enhancing the capacity of the financial intelligence unit.

provisions.title: G-SII Assessment Methodology
provisions.text: The IAIS developed an assessment methodology for identifying global systemically important insurers (G-SIIs). The methodology uses an indicator-based approach covering: size (total assets, total revenues), global activity (revenues derived outside home jurisdiction), interconnectedness (intra-financial system assets and liabilities), non-traditional insurance and non-insurance activities (NTNI), and substitutability. The IAIS has since moved to a holistic framework for assessing and mitigating systemic risk in the insurance sector.

provisions.title: Holistic Framework for Systemic Risk in Insurance
provisions.text: The IAIS holistic framework for assessing and mitigating systemic risk in the insurance sector replaces the entity-based G-SII designation with an activities-based approach. The framework includes: supervisory policy measures targeting specific systemic risk activities (macroprudential surveillance, liquidity risk management, counterparty exposure management, recovery and resolution planning); a global monitoring exercise to assess the build-up of systemic risk; and collective action by supervisors to address identified systemic risks. The framework applies to all insurers, not just those designated as G-SIIs.

provisions.title: Insurance Capital Standard (ICS)
provisions.text: The IAIS is developing a risk-based Insurance Capital Standard (ICS) for internationally active insurance groups (IAIGs). The ICS uses a market-adjusted valuation approach for assets and liabilities, with a current estimate of insurance liabilities plus a margin over current estimate (MOCE). Capital requirements are calibrated to a 99.5% Value-at-Risk over a one-year time horizon. The ICS covers insurance risk, market risk, credit risk, and operational risk. The ICS monitoring period ran from 2020-2024, with implementation as a prescribed capital requirement from 2025.

provisions.title: ICS Risk Charges
provisions.text: The ICS calculates capital requirements across several risk categories: insurance risk (mortality, longevity, morbidity, lapse, expense, catastrophe, premium and claims reserve risk); market risk (interest rate, equity, real estate, currency, asset concentration); credit risk (default and migration risk on financial instruments); and operational risk (based on premium and reserve volumes). Risk charges are aggregated using a correlation matrix that recognises diversification benefits across risk categories.

provisions.title: Common Framework for Supervision of IAIGs (ComFrame)
provisions.text: ComFrame is a set of supervisory requirements focusing on the effective group-wide supervision of internationally active insurance groups (IAIGs). ComFrame builds on the ICPs and specifies additional requirements for IAIGs in areas including: identification of IAIGs; group-wide supervisory process (supervisory colleges, information sharing, host recognition); group governance and enterprise risk management; ORSA at group level; ICS as the group-wide capital requirement; and crisis management and resolution. An IAIG is defined by size (premium above USD 50 billion or assets above USD 200 billion) and international activity (premiums in at least 3 jurisdictions).

provisions.title: IAIS Application Paper on Supervision of Insurer Cybersecurity
provisions.text: The IAIS has published guidance on the supervision of insurer cybersecurity. Key areas include: cyber risk governance (board-level oversight, CISO role); risk identification and assessment (asset inventories, vulnerability assessments, threat intelligence); protective controls (access management, data security, network security); detection and response (monitoring, incident response plans, forensic capability); recovery (business continuity, disaster recovery); and information sharing (cyber threat intelligence sharing with other insurers, authorities, and cross-sector bodies).

provisions.title: IAIS Application Paper on Climate Risk
provisions.text: The IAIS has published guidance on the supervision of climate-related risks in the insurance sector. Insurance-specific considerations include: impact of climate change on insurance underwriting (frequency and severity of natural catastrophe losses, insurability gaps); impact on investment portfolios (stranded assets, physical risks to real estate, transition risks); the role of insurers in climate risk mitigation (risk pricing signals, risk transfer products, investment in climate adaptation); and disclosure of climate-related financial risks. Supervisors should incorporate climate risk into their supervisory frameworks.

fatf_country_status.status: High-Risk Jurisdiction subject to a Call for Action

fatf_country_status.notes: Working to address deficiencies in casino supervision and beneficial ownership.
Committed to address strategic deficiencies in AML/CFT framework.
Working to strengthen supervisory framework and international cooperation.
Working to strengthen national AML/CFT legal framework.
